learning_outcomes.description: Explain different qualitative methods and assess reported qualitative research results.

Understand how to gather and analyse data to present an argument based on qualitative research approaches that address issues of ethical practice, reflexivity, quality criteria and trustworthiness in research results.
Apply qualitative data analysis approaches to your area of business research (e.g., management, marketing, strategy).

Critically discuss the relevance and importance of qualitative methods in business research practice, and be able to communicate and justify your own approach to qualitative research in both written and spoken forms.

assessments.due_date:
Pilot Research Description - 2:00PM - 18/03/2026

Preliminary Coding Report - 2:00PM - 6/05/2026

Presentation of Pilot Findings - During Class - Week 12 Wed - Week 13 Wed
assessments.task_description:
This assessment is designed as a progressive, hands-on inquiry sequence that develops students’ practical understanding of qualitative research. Part A builds foundational skills in research design and question alignment, Part B provides formative feedback on early coding and analytic reasoning, and Part C synthesises preliminary thematic analysis and methodological justification. Together, the components scaffold learning across design, analysis, and communication in qualitative inquiry.

Part A: Pilot Research Description (10%)

Students submit a short pilot research description outlining the purpose and goals of their study. This includes:

  • an introduction and context for the research problem,
  • a brief description of the conceptual or theoretical framework guiding the inquiry, and
  • a rationale for a qualitative research design aligned with the student’s disciplinary foundations.

This formative task provides early feedback on the clarity of research questions and the alignment between research aims, sampling strategy, and interview method.

Due: Week 4 – Wednesday 18 March @ 2:00pm

Part B: Preliminary Coding Report (20%)

This formative assessment provides early feedback on students’ qualitative analysis prior to the final presentation. Students submit a formatted interview transcript, a short interview summary, preliminary coding of the transcript, a draft codebook (codes and definitions), and a coding memo outlining early patterns, analytic decisions, uncertainties, and next steps.

Feedback focuses on the appropriateness of coding decisions, the coherence of code definitions, and the quality of evidence-based analytic reasoning.

Due: Week 10 – Wednesday 6 May @ 2:00pm

Part C: Presentation of Pilot Findings (40%)

Building directly on the Preliminary Coding Report (Part B), students deliver an oral presentation reporting on their qualitative pilot project and developed preliminary analysis. The presentation must demonstrate progression from initial coding to early thematic patterns, with clear alignment between research questions, research design, analytic decisions, and supporting data. Students are also required to explain how quality criteria for qualitative research (e.g., credibility, dependability, reflexivity, transparency) have informed methodological and analytic choices across the inquiry process.

Supporting documentation must be submitted on the presentation day and includes presentation slides, formatted and coded interview transcripts, data analysis tables (codes, definitions, and illustrative excerpts), and a two-page formative data analysis memo.

Due: Presented in Weeks 12–13 during scheduled seminar sessions

Please Note: The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy.

AI Statement:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Purpose of the assessment: This assessment is designed to help students situate qualitative method choices within their discipline’s standards and to use that understanding to design a feasible, credible pilot study.

Students will submit a 2,500-word research paper that critically reviews how qualitative research is conducted, justified, and evaluated within their discipline, drawing on a minimum of 10 influential peer-reviewed qualitative studies from high-quality journals. The paper must demonstrate disciplinary understanding of qualitative inquiry and include a considered discussion of how the qualitative methods planned for the student’s pilot study (and future research) align with disciplinary norms. An abstract of no more than 150 words must be included, along with a reference list, and students must consistently apply the referencing style customary to their discipline (e.g., APA).

In this paper, students will:

Explain how qualitative research is conducted in their discipline

Describe how qualitative studies in the discipline typically collect and analyse data, including commonly used data collection methods and analytic approaches, and how these are justified in published research.

Evaluate the strengths and limitations of qualitative inquiry

Analyse the types of research questions qualitative methods are well suited to in the discipline, as well as their recognised limitations or risks, drawing on disciplinary and social science methods literature.

Identify contemporary challenges and opportunities in qualitative analysis

Summarise key challenges and opportunities in qualitative data collection and analysis, including the use of qualitative data analysis software and emerging AI-supported tools, supported by appropriate scholarly sources.

Explain ethics as a core feature of qualitative research practice

Describe how ethical considerations shape qualitative research in the discipline, including participant protection, consent, confidentiality, power relations, and researcher positioning, and how these considerations inform the design of the student’s planned pilot study.

AI Statement:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
